proposal_text:
Proposal number: 1333885

Proposal: LET'S WALK! - climate change awareness day event

Summary


Purpose: helping to rise the climate change awareness our world so urgently needs. 

Suggested actions: During that day we encourage people to walk and to:

leave their cars at home,
skip the bus,
avoid the subway and
turn to their own bodies in order to move around the city, by walking, by using their bicycles, their rollers, their skates.
If you are not lucky enough to be living near your work space you can still collaborate by doing home office and other activities such as walking to the grocery or the club.
Using a smartphone app we intend to track how much people walk during the day of the event. This information will then be published by social media and traditional media. The data provided by the app will also be translate in the reductions already mentioned, calculating savings in relationship  with the kilometrers walked and avoiding using transportation during that day.The application may use a similar alogorithm that "calculate your ecological footprint" uses, that is available at the Government web page [14].
Because of the altruistic spirit of the cause, we expect to have support from the local ONG´s, health organizations and the government.     
Main value generation loops v1.0.png


Short term Impacts: we expect to save energy and money, GHG emissions, and we will contribute to build sustainable habits, for a healthy environment in the war against the dangers of a sedentary lifestyle.  

Long term impacts:

Since the proposal is to make people aware of how important it is to care about the planet. More climate change aware people would naturally support the following initiatives:

Recycling: We forecast that it could increase by 10 %,
 
Energy savings: recommendations use increment by 10 %
 
Distributed solar photovoltaic electricity law: is approved and covers 5 % of city demand.
 
Electric buses: their use is implemented in 2 % of city bus lines.
Is this proposal for a practice or a project?
Practice

What actions do you propose?
a) The main thrust of “Let’s Walk” is walking, because Buenos Aires has a high population density, and distances might be shorter than in other cities, so walking to work is a real possibility for some people. People can

Walk 10 - 15 blocks to their offices or back home.

Use public transportation, including buses, underground / subways and then walk the rest of the way to their offices or homes.

 

b) Cycling also adds value to the “Let’s walk” proposal for the population 10 to 30 blocks from their office. The Buenos Aires City already has implemented an “ecobici” (eco bikes) program [15]. The ecobici system has bikes parked in several distributed city points, and anyone can use one of these bikes for free using his / her document. You just return the bike in another bike parking place near your destination.

 

REVIEW OF SOME “BIKE TO WORK” PROGRAMS IN CANADA AND USA:

USA Today informs that Bike to Work programs might be rising in US [21].

Through a google search we found the following relevant links:

table-bike-to-work-projects.png


ANSWERS TO SOME OBJECTIONS TO “BIKE TO WORK”

The Huffpost article [16] mentions 9 objections to “Bike to work”, here are our thoughts about them!

main-objections.png

“LET’S WALK BUSINESS MODEL CANVAS”

LET`S WALK PROJECT BUSINESS CANVAS v2.0.png

As we know we have different partners in this event, which play a fundamental role.

On one side, the people enrolled in the event, that will participate registering using a mobile app. They will either walk to their works or commute by bike. In order to be eligible to receive a prize, each participant will allow the app to register information about distances walked / biked, which transportation they usually use to commute in order to calculate GHG emission savings due to the event. Those people that contribute with the biggest GHG emission savings will win prizes as well as  benefits from companies that sponsor the event.

It´s necessary to distinguish, that by incentivizing the movement we are preventing obesity, decrease bad cholesterol and increase good cholesterol, improving the state of mind and incorporating healthy habits, so typically HMOs and the city government will be very much interested, as in Argentina poor people use public hospitals maintained by city taxes.

On the other side, we need to be able to organize this event with which we need the Government's support to convey the event to the society. It will be benefited by an increased use of the public transport by the population, better flow of the traffic (less cars in the streets)  and at the same time it will reaffirm it´s commitment with the environment.

In addition to this, it will be a great opportunity to advertise the use of the eco-bikes(“ecobicis”) [15] and, as a result, the bike lanes.

On the other hand, the information generated by the app (big data)  will be of great utility since it will show which are the population's needs (for example: the best locations for entrances of subways , routes of buses or even which are the areas that are disconnected and need public transport).

It is also important to have the companies that will join on this great event allowing the use of “home office”. In addition, they will collaborate as sponsors and obtain in exchange Big Data to improve its business and find niche.

Sponsorships are one of the best ways of promoting a business and winning popularity between the target and generate good marketing for a brand. It can be specially used to increase credibility, image and prestige of the brand.

All participation is voluntary, and public transportation will still be available to those who want to use it.

LGC Team will ask for suggestions and advice from NGOs(such as WWF, who implemented "The earth hour"). In order to have more tools to guide the team on how to develop the project. 
 
Once LCG Team recieves feedback from NGOs.They will present the formal proposal to the Institute of Technology of Buenos Aires (ITBA) and to the MIT Colab
 
The team is looking forward to receive feedback from CLIMATE COLAB in order to improve the project. ITBA will give their consent and support to it.
 
We received the recognition and prize from MIT Colab thanks to the backing through votes from ITBA.
 
LGC Team will contact several organizations to help with fundraising and to add value to the final event (GOOGLE, MASS MEDIA, SPONSORS, etc). Nevertheless to make it happen we need firstly the GOVERNMENT authorization.
 
The government will notificate the companies of the proposal and ask for their cooperation by allowing the employees to work from home. They will also have to arrange with the transportation about the modification of their routes.
 
Distance walked and GHG savings: To have an estimation of the GHGs saved is necessary an app (develop by GOOGLE if possible) that will measure them.The application may use a similar alogorithm that "calculate your ecological footprint" uses, that is available at the Government web page.But it is important to keep record of the route like Google Maps does. 
 
Once the proposal is accepted the next step is to settle a date and communicate it to the society, a key actor in this process is MASS MEDIA. 
 
The day of the event SPONSORS will play an important role by motivating the population to walk more. This could be achived by offering refreshments and prizes. 
 
Finally,all the information is collected by GOOGLE. Including steps per person and the equivalence with the reduction of GHGs obteined.
 
The LGC Team will analyze the results and make papers to be published and to communicate it to the community, the government and the MIT. 
 
As the results will be positive this idea can be implemented in other countries, all over the world. By this the proposal will reach a greater impact and a change in people's habits worldwide.


Who will take these actions?
LGC Team at ITBA: designs proposal and presents it to Climate Colab.
 
Argentine Government sponsors "LET'S WALK day"
 
Media covers the events publishing metrics on energy saved, emissions saved and money saved.
 
5 % local city population participates in the "LET'S WALK day"
 
NGOs sponsor event.
 
Transportation works as usual.

Other countries can implement the proposal to have a higher impact
Where will these actions be taken?
City level: The first objective is to do it in Buenos Aires City as is the most populated region, the most contaminating one and the principal concentration of companies.

Country level: Later by the positive results our goal is to carry out the experience at country level, in cities like Cordoba, Rosario, Mendoza and San Luis.

Worldwide: Finally, other countries can use the proposal to make their own impacts since everything counts.

 

In addition, specify the country or countries where these actions will be taken.
Argentina

Country 2
Brazil

Country 3
Chile

Country 4
United States

Country 5
No country selected

Impact/Benefits
What impact will these actions have on greenhouse gas emissions and/or adapting to climate change?
1) City of Buenos Aires

a) Short term CO2e emissions saved in Buenos Aires city:

We postulate that 5 % of the population will participate in the event.
Public transportation use: 80% of population [1]

 
Emissions saved by cars commuting from Buenos Aires Province:

10410352 cars/month [2] /30 days/month x 15 km/trip  x 0.1 kg CO2eq / km [3] x 0.05 ( 5 % population participates  in event) = 26 tCO2e (metric tons) / day.
 

Emission saved by cars inside the city:

2.89 million people x 5% people participation x 20 % people use cars x 7 km average trip to work x 2 trips /day x 0.1 kg CO2eq / km [3] = 40.46 tCO2e / day
 

Total short term CO2e savings = (26 + 40.46) = 66.46 tCO2e /day

b) Long term CHG savings (5 years) in Buenos Aires city:

We forecast that recycling could increase by 10 %. Today only 23% of Buenos Aires city recycle [4]. And recycling saves from 0.34 to 10 Tonnes of CO2e from each ton of recycled material [5]. Considering an average 1 Tonne of CO2e saved per Tonne of recycled material, current Buenos Aires savings from recycling would be 976 Tonnes of domiciliary residues [6] x 23% people recycle x 0.1 (10 % recycling increase ) x 1 tCO2e/1 t recycled material = 22 Tonnes of CO2e / year saved.
 
Energy savings recommendations [7] use increment by 10 %. The average electricity consumption per electrified household in Argentina is 3600 kWh [11], so we estimate that the savings will be of 360 kWh/home per year * 963 thousand homes * 0.05 (5 % of participation) = 17334 kWh per year. This could save up to 17334 kWh x 0.0005925 tCO2e/kWh = 10.2 tCO2e/year
Distributed solar photo voltaic electricity law is approved and covers 1 % of city demand.
2.89 million people x 4100 kWh/person [10] x 0.01 (1% of demand) x 0.0005925 tCO2e/kWh = 70205 tCO2e/year
 
It incentivates the use of public transport in this way, so the Government must make it more efficient.For example, the use of electric buses is implemented in 2 % of city.[8] [9] Each bus will save 175 metrics tones of pollutant emissions and 22.700 liters of fuel per unit and year. We estimate that there are 10200 buses in Buenos Aires,so 2% is 204, the total reduction will be 204*175 mtones= 35700 metric tones of pollutant / year saved.[10]
 
Total emission savings = (22 + 10.2 + 70205 + 35700) = 105937 tCO2e/year

2) Extrapolation to world:

a) Short term:

66.46 tCO2e/(2.89million people) x (1087 million people) = 24997 tCO2e

b) Long term:

105.937 tCO2e/year /(2.89million people) x (1087 million people) = 39 million tCO2e / year.

The transportation sector accounts from 19 % to 25 % of total GHGs emissions, according to [12] and [13]

So the total GHG due to the transportation sector may be calculated as

46.000 million tCO2e x 22% = 10.120 million tCO2e

So the proposal savings would be 39 / 10120 = 0.38 % of the transportation sector emissions.

What are other key benefits?
Generate awareness on using more public transport
Government will have to improve the efficiency of public transport
Change the habits of the society towards a sustainable and ecological life
Smog and sound pollution will be reduced
People´s stress levels will be reduced
Increase the use of bicycles
Change habits and weaken sedentary lifestyle
Cut costs of transport and so combustibles
By implemeting home-office besides helping this cause, the employeers will have a cut down of their costs
Decrease GHGs emissions by reducing the use of cars and public transport
Costs/Challenges
What are the proposal’s projected costs?
1) Cost of security forces involved in organizing the event.

2) Cost of health support personnel, ambulances, paramedics,

3) Cost of traditional media ads

4) Cost of blog, SN ads and  social media managers / influencers

5) Cost of winner prizes

6) Cost of collaboration agreement with smartphone apps that calculate distances walked by people participating in the event

7) Cost of webpage that summarizes energy savings, money saved, GHGs emission saved.

Timeline
The day of the event, there will be an immediate impact by the reduction of the GHGs emissions.
In the short-term we are looking forward to make people realize that their can make a huge change on a daily basis.For example, they can avoid the use of cars in short distances.
Over more, we can affirm that one of important change will be the use bikes. Nowadays, Buenos Aires city provides free bikes and bicicles in the downtown. Our idea is to massify these kind of habits and became a green country free of sedentarism.
What's more, we want to Incentive companies to stablish a home-office day. This will allow people who live far from their jobs to colaborate to this cause.
Medium-term: These "Homeoffice days" could at first be once a year, but once people catch the habit, it could be done monthly.
Long-term: Eventually people could do these "Homeoffice days" weekly, so that people enjoy life a little more and savings of energy, money and GHGs emissions are multiplied.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a highly optimistic and unverified assumption that 5% of the city's population (approx. 145,000 people) will voluntarily participate in a single-day event without significant logistical friction. The causal chain requiring government authorization to modify bus routes and compel corporate home-office adoption for a one-day event is technically and politically fragile. Furthermore, the reliance on a custom app developed by a major tech giant (Google) without a confirmed partnership introduces a critical single point of failure.

novelty.rationale: The core concept of 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' days is well-established globally (e.g., Earth Hour, Bike to Work Week). The proposal does not introduce a novel mechanism for behavior change or a new technological approach; it repurposes existing 'ecobici' infrastructure and standard footprint calculation algorithms. The novelty is limited to the specific local context of Buenos Aires, not the intervention itself.

impact.rationale: The short-term impact calculation (66.46 tCO2e/day) is mathematically plausible but negligible in the context of global or even national emissions. The long-term impact projections are scientifically unsound due to a 'attribution error': the proposal attributes massive systemic changes (e.g., 10% increase in recycling, 2% adoption of electric buses, 1% solar coverage) directly to a single awareness day. There is no empirical evidence or causal mechanism provided to support the claim that a one-day walking event will drive multi-year legislative and infrastructure shifts of this magnitude.

presentation.rationale: The proposal contains significant technical inconsistencies and calculation errors. For instance, the long-term impact section cites a solar law covering '1%' of demand in one sentence and '5%' in another. The extrapolation to global impact (39 million tCO2e/year) assumes a linear scaling of a local behavioral nudge to the entire world population, ignoring vast differences in urban density, infrastructure, and culture. The text also contains grammatical errors and informal phrasing that undermine the scientific rigor expected for an empirical evaluation.

A1.perspective_summary: From a systems and empirical evidence perspective, this proposal suffers from a severe disconnect between the intervention (a one-day awareness event) and the projected outcomes (massive systemic infrastructure and legislative changes). While the short-term emission savings are calculable, they are trivial. The long-term impact claims rely on unsupported causal leaps, assuming that a single event will trigger complex, multi-year policy and behavioral shifts without intermediate mechanisms. The feasibility is low due to the lack of confirmed partnerships and the high coordination cost required to mobilize 5% of a major metropolis.
key_concerns: Causal Attribution Error: The proposal incorrectly attributes long-term systemic changes (solar law adoption, electric bus fleet conversion) to a single-day event without a defined causal mechanism or intermediate policy steps.

Participation Uncertainty: The 5% participation rate is an arbitrary assumption with no baseline data or pilot study to support it; mobilizing ~145,000 people for a voluntary event is a high-risk logistical challenge.
Data Integrity: The reliance on a hypothetical app developed by Google and the use of generic footprint algorithms introduces uncertainty in the accuracy of the reported GHG savings.
Scaling Fallacy: The global extrapolation assumes uniform behavior and infrastructure across diverse countries (e.g., US vs. Argentina), which is scientifically invalid.

System Interdependencies: The proposal assumes government and corporate entities will alter operations (bus routes, home office mandates) based on a single event, ignoring bureaucratic inertia and operational constraints.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on unrealistic institutional levers and regulatory assumptions. It assumes the government can mandate or effectively coordinate 'home office' days for private companies and modify public transport routes on short notice without legislative or labor negotiation frameworks. Furthermore, the plan to have Google develop a custom tracking app and the assumption that 5% of the population will voluntarily participate in a single-day event without a robust incentive structure or existing behavioral baseline makes execution highly uncertain. The governance capacity required to synchronize NGOs, media, transport authorities, and private sector employers for a one-off event is not demonstrated.

novelty.rationale: The core concept of 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' days is a well-established global practice (e.g., Earth Hour, Bike to Work Month). The proposal adds little novelty in terms of governance mechanisms or policy innovation. While the integration of a specific app for data tracking is mentioned, the reliance on existing 'ecobici' infrastructure and standard carbon footprint algorithms suggests a replication of existing models rather than a novel governance approach.

impact.rationale: The projected long-term impacts are speculative and lack a credible causal chain. The proposal forecasts a 10% increase in recycling and the approval of a distributed solar law as direct results of a single walking event, which is a significant overestimation of behavioral spillover effects. The short-term GHG savings (66 tCO2e/day) are negligible in the context of city-wide emissions, and the extrapolation to global impacts (39 million tCO2e/year) is mathematically flawed and ignores the complexity of scaling local behavioral nudges to national policy changes.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with clear sections (Purpose, Actions, Impacts, Costs), but the presentation of data is inconsistent and often lacks necessary citations or context. The inclusion of image placeholders (e.g., 'Main value generation loops v1.0.png') without the actual visual data reduces clarity. The tone is optimistic but lacks the rigorous policy framing needed to convince institutional stakeholders of the feasibility of the proposed regulatory interventions.

A2.perspective_summary: From a governance and regulatory perspective, this proposal is an aspirational awareness campaign that lacks the institutional depth to be a viable policy intervention. It conflates voluntary behavioral nudges with mandatory regulatory outcomes (e.g., solar law approval, transport route changes) and assumes a level of cross-sector coordination that is rarely achievable without a pre-existing, robust governance framework. The proposal needs to shift from 'event planning' to 'policy piloting' to be taken seriously by institutional actors.
key_concerns: Regulatory feasibility of mandating or coordinating 'home office' days across private sector employers without labor law implications.
Lack of a clear governance model for data privacy and ownership regarding the proposed smartphone app and 'big data' collection.
Unrealistic causal links between a one-day walking event and long-term structural changes like solar law approval or recycling rate increases.
Absence of a defined accountability mechanism for the government and sponsors if the event fails to meet participation targets.
Unclear funding model for the proposed app development and security/health support costs.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a 'big bang' single-day event model with unrealistic dependencies: government authorization to modify bus routes, Google developing a custom tracking app, and mass corporate adoption of home office for a one-off day. The operational complexity of coordinating security, health support, and media for a city-wide walk without a clear budget or phased pilot is high. The assumption that 5% of the population will participate in a single day without prior behavioral conditioning is operationally optimistic.

novelty.rationale: The core concept (walking/biking to work days) is a well-established global practice (e.g., 'Bike to Work Day', 'Car-Free Day'). The proposal adds a smartphone tracking component, but this is a standard feature in existing fitness and carbon footprint apps. The novelty lies only in the specific local context of Buenos Aires, not in the mechanism itself.

impact.rationale: The short-term impact (66 tCO2e/day) is negligible on a global or even national scale. The long-term impact projections are mathematically flawed; the proposal attributes systemic infrastructure changes (e.g., 2% electric bus fleet, 1% solar coverage) to a single awareness event, which is a causal overreach. While the health benefits of walking are real, the climate impact of a one-day event is transient unless it triggers permanent policy shifts, which the proposal does not detail how to secure.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with clear sections (Business Model Canvas, Impact calculations), but the tone is overly idealistic and lacks critical operational detail. The impact calculations contain significant logical leaps (e.g., attributing solar law approval to a walking event). The reliance on image placeholders (e.g., 'main-objections.png') without text descriptions in the provided summary reduces clarity. It reads more like a concept pitch than an implementation plan.

A3.perspective_summary: From an implementation standpoint, this proposal is a classic 'awareness event' that lacks the operational scaffolding to be scalable or sustainable. It treats complex systemic changes (transport infrastructure, energy laws) as direct outputs of a single-day behavioral nudge. The resource requirements (custom app dev, government route changes) are disproportionate to the likely outcome. It needs to pivot from a 'one-day spectacle' to a recurring, low-friction program with clear, incremental KPIs.
key_concerns: Dependency on Google to develop a custom app is a critical single point of failure; no budget or technical partner is confirmed.
The causal link between a one-day walk and long-term infrastructure changes (solar laws, electric bus fleets) is logically unsound and inflates impact scores.
Operational risk: Coordinating security, medical support, and traffic management for 5% of a city's population on a single day is a massive logistical undertaking with no clear funding source.
Adoption barrier: Convincing employers to mandate or strongly encourage home office for a single day is difficult without a pre-existing corporate culture of remote work.
Data integrity: Relying on voluntary self-reporting via an app for GHG calculations introduces significant noise and potential for gaming the system.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on unrealistic assumptions about government and corporate behavior, specifically that the government will 'arrange with transportation about the modification of their routes' and that companies will universally adopt home office policies for a single day without a binding mandate. The technical feasibility of a custom app to track routes and calculate GHG savings is also questionable given the reliance on 'Google' to develop it and the lack of a clear data privacy framework for the 'big data' collection.

novelty.rationale: The core concept of a 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' day is well-established globally (e.g., Earth Hour, Bike to Work Day). While the proposal attempts to add novelty through a specific app and data tracking mechanism, the fundamental approach of a one-day awareness event is not new. The 'novelty' lies more in the specific local context of Buenos Aires, but the mechanism itself is a standard awareness campaign.

impact.rationale: The short-term impact is negligible (66 tCO2e/day) and the long-term projections (39 million tCO2e/year) are mathematically flawed and overly optimistic, assuming a one-day event will permanently alter global habits and infrastructure. From a community perspective, the proposal risks 'slacktivism' where a single day of walking does not translate to sustained behavior change or structural improvements in public transport accessibility for those who cannot walk or bike due to distance or disability.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with clear sections and attempts to use data, but the tone is often naive and the calculations contain significant logical leaps (e.g., extrapolating a one-day event to global annual savings). The inclusion of 'Business Model Canvas' and 'Review of Objections' shows effort, but the text is cluttered with broken image references and inconsistent formatting, which undermines the professional credibility needed to secure the necessary government and corporate partnerships.

A4.perspective_summary: From the ground, this proposal feels like a well-intentioned but disconnected top-down initiative that underestimates the daily realities of the working class and the physically vulnerable. While the idea of walking is healthy, the proposal assumes a level of flexibility in work schedules and housing proximity that many residents of Buenos Aires simply do not have. The heavy reliance on a smartphone app for tracking creates a digital divide, excluding those without data plans or devices, and the promise of 'big data' to solve infrastructure issues feels like a distraction from the immediate need for reliable, accessible public transit.
key_concerns: The proposal assumes all participants can walk or bike to work, ignoring those with disabilities, chronic illnesses, or long commutes that make active transport impossible.
The 'big data' collection via a smartphone app raises significant privacy concerns and excludes low-income populations who may not own smartphones or have data plans.
The assumption that the government will modify bus routes and companies will mandate home office days for a one-day event is highly unlikely without a long-term policy framework.
The long-term impact calculations are mathematically unsound, extrapolating a single day's behavior to global annual savings without evidence of habit retention.
The proposal focuses on 'awareness' rather than addressing the structural barriers (safety, infrastructure, cost) that prevent people from walking or biking daily.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a highly optimistic and unverified assumption that the government will mandate or strongly enforce 'home office' days and modify public transport routes for a single event. The logistical burden of coordinating security, health support, and mass media for a voluntary event without a clear operational plan for the 'government authorization' bottleneck makes execution risky. Furthermore, the reliance on a Google-developed app and specific corporate sponsorships introduces significant dependency risks that are not mitigated by a concrete fallback plan.

novelty.rationale: The core concept of 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' days is a well-established global practice, not a novel innovation. The proposal explicitly references existing programs in the US and Canada and the local 'Ecobici' system. While the integration of a specific tracking app and the attempt to link data to long-term policy changes (solar law, electric buses) adds a layer of ambition, the fundamental mechanism is a standard awareness campaign rather than a new model of intervention.

impact.rationale: The proposal identifies clear value generation loops regarding health, awareness, and potential long-term habit formation. However, the quantitative impact claims are deeply flawed and ethically problematic due to 'attribution errors.' The proposal credits the event with long-term structural changes (e.g., 5% city demand covered by solar, 2% electric bus implementation) that are policy outcomes, not direct results of a one-day walk. While the short-term emission savings (66 tCO2e) are real but negligible on a global scale, the proposal hides the trade-off of potentially diverting attention from systemic policy changes by focusing on individual behavioral shifts that may not be sustainable without structural support.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with clear sections and attempts to use data, but the presentation of the 'Impact' section is misleading. It conflates correlation with causation, presenting long-term policy forecasts (solar law approval, electric bus rollout) as direct outcomes of the 'Let's Walk' event. The text contains grammatical errors and inconsistent formatting (e.g., image placeholders without context), which undermines the professional credibility required to secure the high-level government and corporate partnerships it demands.

A5.perspective_summary: This proposal attempts to leverage a one-day behavioral nudge to claim credit for complex, long-term systemic policy shifts, creating a significant gap between the intervention and the promised impact. From an equity and justice lens, the proposal risks 'greenwashing' by attributing structural policy wins to a voluntary event, potentially obscuring the need for mandatory, systemic regulation. The reliance on voluntary participation and corporate goodwill without addressing the exclusion of those who cannot walk or work from home raises concerns about who actually benefits versus who is left behind.
key_concerns: Attribution Error: The proposal falsely credits a one-day event with long-term policy outcomes (solar law, electric bus implementation) that are independent of the event's success.

Exclusion Risk: The proposal assumes all citizens can walk or bike to work, ignoring those with disabilities, caregiving responsibilities, or jobs requiring physical presence, potentially stigmatizing those who cannot participate.
Data Privacy & Equity: The reliance on a smartphone app to track movement and award prizes creates a digital divide, excluding low-income populations who may lack smartphones or data plans.
Government Dependency: The feasibility hinges on government mandates (home office, route changes) that are politically volatile and not guaranteed, creating a high risk of project failure.
Value Conflict: The proposal prioritizes 'awareness' and 'marketing' for sponsors over the immediate, tangible needs of vulnerable populations who rely on public transport for survival.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a highly optimistic and unproven causal chain where a single-day awareness event triggers systemic policy shifts (e.g., 2% electric bus fleet conversion, 1% solar law coverage) and behavioral changes (10% recycling increase) over five years. The assumption that a one-off 'Walk Day' will compel governments to restructure public transit routes or pass energy laws lacks a credible implementation pathway. Furthermore, the reliance on a 'Google-developed' app and immediate government authorization for a massive behavioral shift without a pilot phase suggests a significant gap between the event concept and the complex logistical reality of city-wide coordination.

novelty.rationale: The core concept of 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' days is a well-established global practice, not a novel innovation. While the proposal attempts to add novelty through data tracking and 'big data' analysis for urban planning, the fundamental mechanism (a one-day voluntary event) is a standard awareness tactic. The proposal does not introduce a new systemic lever or a novel approach to overcoming the structural barriers of urban mobility; it merely repackages existing awareness campaigns with a data-tracking layer.

impact.rationale: From a long-term resilience perspective, the projected impacts are likely overstated and potentially misleading. The proposal conflates a single day's emission reduction (66 tCO2e) with long-term structural changes (105,000+ tCO2e/year) that are attributed to the event but are actually dependent on independent policy decisions (solar laws, electric bus procurement) that a walking event cannot guarantee. True long-term impact requires shifting from 'event-based' awareness to 'systemic' habit formation; however, the proposal assumes that a one-day event will automatically lead to weekly home-office days and major infrastructure overhauls without addressing the economic or structural incentives required to sustain these changes. The risk is 'problem shifting' where the event creates a temporary illusion of progress without building the resilience needed for permanent decarbonization.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with clear sections and attempts to use data to support its claims, which is commendable. However, the presentation suffers from a lack of critical realism in its impact modeling. The jump from a one-day event to specific, quantified long-term policy outcomes (e.g., 'Distributed solar photovoltaic electricity law: is approved') is presented as a forecast rather than a conditional possibility, which undermines the credibility of the long-term analysis. The inclusion of business model canvases and objection reviews adds depth, but the narrative fails to distinguish between the event's direct output and the broader, unrelated policy environment.

A6.perspective_summary: This proposal treats a complex, systemic challenge (urban decarbonization and lifestyle change) as a solvable problem through a single-day awareness event. While the intent to build health and environmental awareness is positive, the long-term impact model is flawed because it assumes a direct, linear causality between a one-off event and major structural policy shifts. True resilience requires addressing the systemic barriers to walking and cycling (infrastructure, safety, land use) rather than relying on voluntary, temporary behavioral changes that may not persist without deep structural support.
key_concerns: The proposal assumes that a one-day event will directly cause the approval of specific laws (solar, electric buses) and significant increases in recycling rates, which is a logical leap not supported by evidence.
There is a risk of 'awareness fatigue' where participants feel they have 'done their part' on the event day, potentially reducing long-term engagement rather than building sustained habits.
The calculation of long-term GHG savings attributes independent policy outcomes (like the solar law) to the event, inflating the perceived impact of the intervention.
The proposal lacks a strategy for maintaining momentum after the event; without a transition from 'event' to 'institutionalized practice,' the long-term societal effects will likely be negligible.
Reliance on a single-day data spike to inform long-term urban planning (big data for transit routes) may yield skewed results that do not reflect normal commuting patterns.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal rests on a fragile causal chain requiring high-level institutional coordination (government route changes, Google app development, corporate home-office mandates) for a single-day event. As noted by peers A2 and A3, the assumption that a one-off awareness day can trigger complex regulatory or corporate behavioral shifts lacks empirical support. The technical feasibility of a custom app developed by a major tech giant for a volunteer event is highly improbable without a formal RFP or budget, and the logistical burden of managing 5% of a city's population (approx. 145,000 people) on foot simultaneously presents significant safety and traffic management risks.

novelty.rationale: The core mechanism—'Walk/Bike to Work' days—is a mature, globally replicated intervention (e.g., Earth Hour, Bike to Work Month). The proposal's attempt to differentiate via a smartphone app for data tracking does not constitute a novel scientific or systems innovation, as similar tracking and carbon calculation algorithms are already commoditized in existing fitness and sustainability platforms. The integration of 'big data' for urban planning is a standard concept, not a novel application in this context.

impact.rationale: The short-term GHG reduction (approx. 66 tCO2e/day) is statistically negligible relative to the city's total emissions. More critically, the long-term impact projections (e.g., 10% recycling increase, solar law approval, 2% electric bus fleet adoption) represent a severe causal overreach. There is no empirical evidence to support a direct causal link between a single-day walking event and the passage of complex legislation or systemic infrastructure upgrades. The extrapolation of local behavioral data to global emission savings (39 million tCO2e/year) relies on flawed linear scaling assumptions that ignore systemic inertia and policy complexity.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with standard sections (Purpose, Actions, Impacts), but the presentation of evidence is weak. It relies heavily on image placeholders (e.g., 'Main value generation loops v1.0.png') which are inaccessible, and the mathematical derivations for long-term impacts contain logical leaps that undermine scientific credibility. While the intent is clear, the lack of rigorous data citation and the optimistic tone without hedging for uncertainty reduces its persuasiveness as a technical proposal.

A1.perspective_summary: From a systems and empirical evidence perspective, this proposal functions as a well-intentioned awareness campaign rather than a scientifically robust intervention. The causal mechanisms linking a single-day behavioral nudge to long-term structural policy changes (solar laws, bus electrification) are unsupported by evidence. The proposal suffers from 'solutionism,' assuming that data collection and a one-off event can bypass the complex institutional and economic barriers required for systemic decarbonization. The technical and logistical dependencies (Google app, government route changes) are unrealistic for a volunteer-led initiative.
key_concerns: Causal overreach: No empirical basis for linking a one-day event to long-term legislative outcomes (solar law) or infrastructure shifts (electric bus fleet).
Technical feasibility: High uncertainty regarding the development of a custom tracking app by a major tech provider (Google) without a formal contract or budget.
Systemic inertia: The proposal underestimates the complexity of coordinating private sector labor policies (home office) and public transport route modifications on short notice.
Data validity: Voluntary self-reporting via an app introduces significant noise and potential for gaming, compromising the integrity of the 'big data' claims.
Scalability: Linear extrapolation of local behavioral data to global emission savings ignores the non-linear nature of systemic adoption and policy diffusion.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal's feasibility is critically undermined by its reliance on high-level political and corporate coordination for a single-day event. The requirement for the government to 'modify bus routes' and compel companies to adopt 'home office' for a one-off event represents a significant institutional overreach that is politically unrealistic without a long-term legislative framework. As noted by peers, the assumption that a voluntary awareness day can trigger mandatory operational shifts in public transit and private enterprise ignores bureaucratic inertia and the lack of a phased implementation strategy. The dependency on a custom app from a major tech giant (Google) without a confirmed partnership further reduces feasibility to a theoretical level.

novelty.rationale: The intervention is a repackaging of established global practices ('Bike to Work', 'Car-Free Day') with no novel governance mechanisms or policy instruments. While the proposal attempts to integrate data tracking, the underlying logic of using a smartphone app to measure carbon footprints is standard and already available in existing municipal and private sector tools. The novelty is confined to the specific local context of Buenos Aires rather than the design of the intervention itself, offering no new insights into how to govern behavioral change at scale.

impact.rationale: The projected impact suffers from severe attribution errors that render the long-term claims invalid from a policy evaluation standpoint. The proposal incorrectly attributes complex, multi-year systemic outcomes (e.g., the approval of a Distributed Solar Photovoltaic law, a 2% shift to electric buses) to a single-day awareness event. There is no defined policy pathway or causal mechanism linking a 'Walk Day' to legislative changes or infrastructure overhauls. The short-term emission savings (approx. 66 tCO2e) are negligible in the context of the city's total emissions, and the extrapolation to global impact relies on flawed linear scaling assumptions that ignore local governance and infrastructure constraints.

presentation.rationale: The presentation fails to meet the standards of a viable policy proposal due to internal inconsistencies and a lack of operational rigor. The text contains contradictory figures regarding solar energy coverage (1% vs. 5%) and relies on placeholder images for critical data. The tone is overly idealistic, presenting a 'wish list' of outcomes rather than a grounded implementation plan. The failure to distinguish between the event's direct outputs and the long-term systemic changes it hopes to influence undermines the credibility of the entire proposal.

A2.perspective_summary: From a governance and regulatory perspective, this proposal is a classic example of 'policy wishful thinking,' where a low-cost, low-friction event is expected to drive high-cost, high-complexity systemic changes without a defined institutional pathway. The proposal lacks the necessary regulatory levers, stakeholder alignment, and phased implementation strategy to be feasible. It conflates awareness with action and assumes that political will can be generated instantaneously to alter public transit routes and corporate work policies. Without a shift from a 'one-day spectacle' to a structured, recurring program with clear policy hooks, the proposal remains institutionally unviable.
key_concerns: Institutional Mismatch: The proposal demands immediate, high-level government and corporate operational changes (route modifications, home office mandates) for a single event, which is politically and administratively unfeasible without prior legislative or policy groundwork.

Causal Attribution Error: The proposal falsely claims that a one-day event will directly cause long-term legislative and infrastructure changes (solar laws, electric bus fleets) without defining the intermediate policy steps or advocacy mechanisms required.

Lack of Governance Capacity: There is no evidence of a governing body or coalition capable of coordinating the necessary security, health, and media logistics for 5% of a major metropolis, nor a budget to support these activities.

Data and Accountability Risks: Relying on a hypothetical app from a third party (Google) for official GHG reporting creates significant data integrity and accountability gaps, as there is no verified methodology or audit trail for the claimed savings.

Scalability Fallacy: The proposal assumes that a local behavioral nudge can be linearly scaled to a global level, ignoring the vast differences in urban governance, infrastructure, and cultural norms across different countries.

feasibility.rationale: Operational feasibility is critically low. The proposal requires a 'perfect storm' of coordination: government authorization to alter bus routes, private sector mandates for home-office days, and a custom app developed by a major tech giant (Google) without a signed partnership. As noted by peers, the assumption that 5% of a major metropolis (145k+ people) will voluntarily participate in a single day without a pre-existing incentive structure or pilot data is a high-risk logistical gamble. The reliance on unconfirmed government and corporate buy-in creates a single point of failure that makes immediate rollout impossible.

novelty.rationale: The intervention is a standard replication of existing 'Bike to Work' and 'Walk to Work' campaigns seen globally (e.g., Earth Hour, local eco-bike initiatives). The proposed 'innovation'—a smartphone app to track steps and calculate footprints—uses existing algorithms and relies on infrastructure (Ecobici) that is already in place. There is no novel mechanism for behavior change, governance, or technology; it is a rebranding of established awareness tactics.

impact.rationale: While the short-term emission savings (approx. 66 tCO2e/day) are mathematically calculable, they are negligible in the context of city-wide emissions. The long-term impact projections suffer from severe attribution errors, claiming that a one-day event will directly cause complex systemic shifts like a 10% increase in recycling, the approval of solar laws, and the electrification of bus fleets. There is no operational pathway or causal mechanism provided to link a single awareness day to these multi-year legislative and infrastructure outcomes.

presentation.rationale: The proposal lacks the rigor required for operational evaluation. It contains internal inconsistencies (e.g., solar law coverage cited as both 1% and 5%), relies on image placeholders instead of data, and presents speculative global extrapolations as facts. The tone is overly optimistic, ignoring the bureaucratic inertia and resource constraints that would block the proposed government and corporate actions. The 'Business Model Canvas' and cost sections are vague, failing to address the significant costs of security, health support, and app development.

A3.perspective_summary: From an implementation and operational feasibility lens, this proposal is a high-risk, low-reward event plan masquerading as a systemic solution. It fails to account for the massive coordination costs, regulatory hurdles, and resource requirements needed to mobilize 5% of a city's population and alter corporate/government operations. The disconnect between the simple intervention (a walk day) and the massive projected outcomes (legislative change, infrastructure overhaul) renders the impact claims scientifically and operationally unsound. Without a confirmed pilot, secured partnerships, and a realistic causal model, this cannot be scaled or implemented as described.
key_concerns: Unsecured Dependencies: The plan hinges on unconfirmed commitments from Google (app dev), the Government (route changes), and private employers (home office mandates).
Logistical Bottleneck: Mobilizing 145,000+ participants for a single day without a pre-existing community base or incentive structure is a massive operational risk.
Causal Fallacy: No operational mechanism exists to translate a one-day event into long-term policy changes like solar law approval or bus fleet electrification.
Resource Gap: The proposal lists costs for security, health, and media but provides no funding strategy or budget breakdown, assuming 'altruistic spirit' will cover operational realities.
Data Integrity: Reliance on a hypothetical app and generic algorithms for GHG calculation introduces significant uncertainty in the reported metrics.

feasibility.rationale: While the event itself is logistically simple, the proposal's feasibility collapses on the ground due to its reliance on government mandates for 'home office' and route changes, which are politically volatile. As A5 and A6 noted, the assumption that a voluntary event can trigger these structural shifts is unrealistic. From a community perspective, the lack of a fallback plan for those who cannot walk (due to disability, distance, or caregiving) makes the 'voluntary' nature of the event exclusionary in practice, reducing actual participation feasibility among the most vulnerable.

novelty.rationale: The concept is a standard 'Walk to Work' campaign, a well-worn tactic in Buenos Aires and globally. The addition of a tracking app and 'big data' claims does not fundamentally alter the intervention model. As my peers correctly identified, this is a repackaging of existing awareness campaigns rather than a novel approach to solving the structural barriers of urban mobility.

impact.rationale: The short-term impact of 66 tCO2e is negligible and likely temporary. The long-term impact claims are deeply flawed because they attribute independent policy outcomes (solar law, electric bus rollout) to a one-day walk. From the ground, this creates a dangerous 'greenwashing' effect where the community feels they have contributed to systemic change through a single day of walking, while the actual structural barriers (unsafe sidewalks, lack of bike lanes, long commutes) remain unaddressed. The proposal risks creating 'awareness fatigue' rather than sustainable habit change.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured but suffers from a lack of critical realism. It presents correlation as causation, claiming the event will cause the approval of laws and infrastructure changes. The text contains grammatical errors and relies on image placeholders that obscure the actual logic. While the intent is clear, the presentation fails to distinguish between the event's direct output and the broader, unrelated policy environment, which undermines its credibility with the very stakeholders (government, NGOs) it needs to succeed.

A4.perspective_summary: This proposal attempts to solve systemic urban mobility and climate issues with a one-day behavioral nudge, ignoring the lived reality of those who cannot simply 'walk to work' due to distance, disability, or safety concerns. The peers' critiques on attribution errors and exclusion risks are spot-on; the proposal risks alienating the very populations it claims to help by framing structural failures as individual choices. The 'big data' promise is a distraction from the lack of a concrete plan to improve the actual walking environment.
key_concerns: Exclusion of vulnerable populations: The proposal assumes a 'one-size-fits-all' ability to walk or bike, ignoring those with disabilities, elderly residents, or those with long commutes who rely on public transit.
Safety and Infrastructure: The proposal encourages walking in a city where sidewalk conditions and traffic safety are major concerns, without addressing the need for safer infrastructure first.
Digital Divide: Relying on a smartphone app for tracking and prizes excludes low-income residents who may not have data plans or devices, creating a two-tiered participation model.

Misleading Impact Metrics: Attributing long-term policy wins (solar law, electric buses) to a single event misleads the community about the true drivers of climate action and distracts from necessary advocacy.
Sustainability of Habits: Without addressing the root causes of car dependency (e.g., urban sprawl, lack of transit), a one-day event is unlikely to create lasting behavioral change.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal's feasibility is critically undermined by its exclusionary design and unrealistic governance assumptions. As peers A4 and A6 noted, the reliance on a smartphone app for tracking creates a digital divide, effectively excluding low-income populations, the elderly, and those without data plans. Furthermore, the assumption that a single-day event can compel government route modifications or corporate home-office mandates ignores the structural inertia of urban planning and labor laws. From a justice perspective, a plan that requires participants to have specific technological access and flexible work arrangements is not feasible for the majority of the population it claims to serve.

novelty.rationale: The core mechanism—a one-day 'Walk to Work' event—is a well-established global practice with no novel structural innovation. The proposal attempts to layer 'big data' collection on top of this standard awareness campaign, but as peers A4 and A6 highlighted, this does not constitute a new solution to the systemic barriers of urban mobility. The novelty is superficial; it repackages a familiar awareness tactic without addressing the underlying value conflicts or exclusion risks inherent in one-off events.

impact.rationale: The projected impact is ethically problematic due to its 'slacktivism' nature and flawed attribution. The proposal attributes long-term structural policy outcomes (e.g., solar law approval, electric bus fleets) to a single-day awareness event, which is a normative leap that obscures the actual drivers of change. More critically, the impact is unevenly distributed: it benefits those with the privilege to walk or bike (proximity to work, physical ability, smartphone ownership) while placing the burden of 'awareness' on the vulnerable without offering them viable alternatives. The short-term emission savings are negligible compared to the systemic changes required, and the long-term projections rely on a false causality that risks misleading stakeholders about the efficacy of such interventions.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with clear sections and attempts to use data, but the presentation fails to address the normative trade-offs and exclusion risks identified by peers. The tone is overly optimistic and naive, presenting speculative long-term policy shifts as direct consequences of the event. While the inclusion of business model canvases shows effort, the text lacks a critical reflection on who is excluded by the app-based tracking and the assumption of flexible work, which weakens its credibility as a serious sustainability intervention.

A5.perspective_summary: From an ethics and justice lens, this proposal hides significant value conflicts under the guise of 'altruism.' It prioritizes the visibility of the event over the actual inclusion of the most vulnerable populations, relying on a digital tracking mechanism that excludes the poor and a behavioral assumption that ignores the structural realities of the working class. The proposal conflates a one-day symbolic gesture with systemic change, risking the perpetuation of 'awareness fatigue' while failing to address the root causes of exclusion in urban mobility. The peers' critiques of the flawed impact modeling and unrealistic governance assumptions are entirely consistent with a justice-focused evaluation, confirming that the proposal's current form is ethically and practically weak.
key_concerns: The proposal excludes low-income populations and those without smartphones via its mandatory app-based tracking, violating principles of inclusion and equitable access.
The assumption that a one-day event can drive complex policy changes (solar laws, bus routes) obscures the real political and economic barriers to decarbonization.
The 'big data' collection raises privacy concerns and risks commodifying the behavior of participants without ensuring data sovereignty or benefit-sharing for the community.
The proposal fails to account for the physical and economic barriers (distance, disability, cost) that prevent many from walking or biking, effectively blaming individuals for structural failures.
Attributing long-term structural outcomes to a single-day event creates a false narrative of impact, potentially diverting resources from more effective, systemic interventions.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal's feasibility is critically undermined by its reliance on a 'one-day' event to trigger complex, long-term systemic shifts (e.g., government route modifications, corporate home-office mandates). As peers A4 and A5 noted, the assumption that a single awareness day can alter infrastructure or policy is structurally unsound. From a resilience perspective, the plan lacks a pathway for sustained engagement; it depends entirely on external actors (Google, Government) without a robust, self-sustaining community mechanism to maintain momentum once the event concludes.

novelty.rationale: The core mechanism—a 'Walk to Work' day with app-based tracking—is a well-established global practice (e.g., Earth Hour, Bike to Work Day). While the proposal attempts to layer 'big data' analytics on top, this does not constitute a novel systemic intervention. The approach remains a standard awareness campaign rather than an innovative model for long-term behavioral or structural change.

impact.rationale: The long-term impact projections are fundamentally flawed due to severe attribution errors. The proposal credits a single day of walking with multi-year policy outcomes (e.g., 5% solar coverage, 2% electric bus adoption) that are independent of the event. This 'slacktivism' approach risks shifting focus away from the necessary, difficult work of systemic policy reform. The short-term emission savings (66 tCO2e) are negligible in the context of global climate goals, and the proposal fails to demonstrate how a one-off event builds the intergenerational resilience required for true sustainability.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with clear sections and attempts to use data, but the presentation of impact is misleading. It conflates correlation with causation, presenting long-term policy forecasts as direct outcomes of the event. The text contains grammatical errors and broken image references, which undermines the professional credibility needed to secure the high-level partnerships required for such an ambitious (yet flawed) vision.

A6.perspective_summary: From a long-term systems lens, this proposal represents a classic case of 'shifting the problem' rather than solving it. It attempts to use a short-term behavioral nudge to claim credit for deep structural changes, which is a dangerous precedent for sustainability planning. The lack of a mechanism to sustain habit formation beyond the event day means the proposal fails to build genuine societal resilience. The reliance on voluntary participation and corporate goodwill ignores the systemic barriers (inequality, infrastructure deficits) that prevent the most vulnerable from participating, potentially exacerbating social divides under the guise of environmental awareness.
key_concerns: The proposal commits a severe attribution error by linking a one-day event to long-term policy outcomes (solar laws, electric bus fleets) that require years of legislative and infrastructural work.
There is no strategy for habit retention; without a sustained engagement loop, the 'awareness' gained on the event day will likely dissipate, yielding zero long-term ecological benefit.

The reliance on a smartphone app for tracking and rewards creates a digital divide, excluding low-income populations and those without data access, thereby failing the test of intergenerational equity.

The proposal assumes a level of flexibility in work and housing (walking distance) that does not exist for a significant portion of the population, risking the stigmatization of those who cannot participate.
The 'big data' promise is a distraction; without a clear plan for how this data translates into *mandatory* policy changes rather than just 'awareness,' the systemic impact remains theoretical.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal is operationally unviable due to its reliance on a 'perfect storm' of unconfirmed high-level dependencies: government authorization to alter bus routes, corporate mandates for home-office days, and the development of a custom tracking app by a major tech giant (Google) without a formal contract. The logistical challenge of mobilizing 5% of a major metropolis (approx. 145,000 people) for a single day without a pre-existing incentive structure or pilot data presents a critical single point of failure.

novelty.rationale: The intervention is a direct replication of mature, globally established practices such as 'Bike to Work' and 'Car-Free Day' campaigns. The proposed differentiator—a smartphone app for tracking steps and calculating carbon footprints—utilizes commoditized algorithms and existing infrastructure (e.g., Ecobici) rather than introducing novel scientific, technological, or governance mechanisms.

impact.rationale: While short-term emission savings are mathematically calculable, they are statistically negligible relative to city-wide totals. The long-term impact projections suffer from severe causal overreach, falsely attributing complex, multi-year systemic outcomes (e.g., passage of solar laws, 2% electric bus fleet adoption, 10% recycling increase) to a single-day awareness event. The extrapolation of local behavioral data to global savings relies on flawed linear scaling assumptions that ignore systemic inertia.

presentation.rationale: The proposal fails to meet standards for a viable policy document due to internal inconsistencies (e.g., conflicting figures on solar energy coverage), reliance on inaccessible image placeholders for critical data, and a lack of rigorous citation. The tone is overly idealistic, presenting a 'wish list' of outcomes without a grounded implementation plan, budget breakdown, or risk mitigation strategy.

S1.synthesis: The cluster (A1, A2, A3) reached a unanimous consensus that the proposal is fundamentally flawed in its causal logic and operational feasibility. All three experts identified the same critical failure points: the unrealistic expectation that a one-off event can trigger complex legislative and infrastructure changes, the dependency on unsecured partnerships with major entities (Google, Government), and the lack of a realistic budget or governance structure. The synthesis integrates A1's focus on 'solutionism' and empirical evidence, A2's critique of 'policy wishful thinking' and institutional mismatch, and A3's assessment of operational bottlenecks. There was no divergence in scoring; the low scores reflect a shared view that the proposal conflates awareness with systemic action and lacks the necessary rigor to be considered a viable intervention.
S1.uncertainty: The primary uncertainty lies in the potential for the proposal to be re-scoped as a purely symbolic awareness campaign rather than a systemic solution. If the long-term impact claims (solar laws, bus electrification) were removed and the scope limited to the immediate, short-term behavioral nudge, the feasibility and impact scores might improve slightly, though the novelty would remain low. Additionally, the specific costs of security and health support for 145,000 participants remain unquantified, leaving the true financial barrier to entry undefined.

feasibility.rationale: The cluster unanimously agrees that feasibility is critically low. While the event logistics are simple, the proposal relies on unrealistic assumptions: that a single-day voluntary event can compel government route modifications, corporate home-office mandates, and infrastructure changes. Furthermore, the mandatory smartphone app for tracking creates a significant digital divide, excluding low-income populations, the elderly, and those without data plans, rendering the 'voluntary' nature exclusionary in practice.

novelty.rationale: All experts concur that the core concept is a repackaging of well-established global campaigns (e.g., 'Bike to Work', 'Earth Hour'). The addition of a tracking app and 'big data' claims does not constitute a novel structural intervention or a new solution to urban mobility barriers. It remains a standard awareness tactic without innovative mechanisms for long-term behavioral or systemic change.

impact.rationale: The projected impact is deemed ethically and analytically flawed. The short-term emission savings (approx. 66 tCO2e) are negligible. More critically, the proposal commits severe attribution errors by claiming credit for independent long-term policy outcomes (solar law approval, electric bus rollout) that are unrelated to a one-day walk. This 'slacktivism' approach risks creating 'awareness fatigue' and misleading stakeholders about the efficacy of the intervention, while failing to address the root causes of car dependency.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structurally organized with clear sections and attempts to use data, earning a moderate score. However, the presentation undermines its own credibility by conflating correlation with causation, presenting speculative policy shifts as direct event outcomes. The text contains grammatical errors and relies on broken image placeholders, which weakens its professional standing with the high-level stakeholders (government, NGOs) required for success.

S2.synthesis: The cluster (A4, A5, A6) reached a high degree of consensus, with all experts assigning identical scores across all dimensions. The synthesis integrates three complementary lenses: A4's focus on community exclusion and safety, A5's emphasis on social justice and the digital divide, and A6's systems perspective on resilience and attribution errors. The collective judgment is that while the intent is altruistic, the proposal fails to account for structural realities (infrastructure, inequality, policy inertia). The 'big data' component is viewed uniformly as a distraction from the lack of a concrete plan to improve the actual walking environment or ensure equitable participation. The proposal is seen as a symbolic gesture that risks alienating vulnerable populations rather than driving systemic change.
S2.uncertainty: The primary uncertainty lies in the potential for the 'big data' component to be repurposed by the government for genuine infrastructure planning if the event were to evolve beyond a one-day nudge. However, the current proposal lacks a mechanism to ensure this data leads to mandatory policy changes rather than just awareness. Additionally, while the scores are unanimous, the specific threshold for 'negligible' impact (66 tCO2e) could be debated if the event were scaled to a global level, though the attribution errors would remain a critical flaw regardless of scale.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal is operationally unviable due to its reliance on a 'perfect storm' of unconfirmed high-level dependencies: government authorization to alter bus routes, corporate mandates for home-office days, and the development of a custom tracking app by a major tech giant (Google) without a formal contract. The logistical challenge of mobilizing 5% of a major metropolis for a single day without a pre-existing incentive structure presents a critical single point of failure.

novelty.rationale: The intervention is a direct replication of mature, globally established practices such as 'Bike to Work' and 'Car-Free Day' campaigns. The proposed differentiator—a smartphone app for tracking steps and calculating carbon footprints—utilizes commoditized algorithms and existing infrastructure rather than introducing novel scientific, technological, or governance mechanisms.

impact.rationale: While short-term emission savings are mathematically calculable, they are statistically negligible relative to city-wide totals. The long-term impact projections suffer from severe causal overreach, falsely attributing complex, multi-year systemic outcomes (e.g., passage of solar laws, electric bus fleet adoption) to a single-day awareness event. The extrapolation of local behavioral data to global savings relies on flawed linear scaling assumptions.

presentation.rationale: Although the proposal is structurally organized, it fails to meet professional standards due to internal inconsistencies (e.g., conflicting figures on solar energy coverage), reliance on inaccessible image placeholders for critical data, and a lack of rigorous citation. The tone is overly idealistic, presenting a 'wish list' of outcomes without a grounded implementation plan, budget breakdown, or risk mitigation strategy.

final.synthesis: The final judgment integrates the unanimous consensus from both clusters (S1 and S2) that the proposal is fundamentally flawed in its causal logic and operational feasibility. Both groups identified the unrealistic expectation that a one-off event can trigger complex legislative and infrastructure changes, and the dependency on unsecured partnerships. While S2 highlighted the social justice implications of the digital divide (excluding those without smartphones) and S1 focused on the 'solutionism' of the app, these perspectives converge to reinforce the low feasibility score. The presentation score was adjusted to 2 (down from S2's 3) to align with S1's critique of the document's internal inconsistencies and lack of professional rigor, ensuring the final judgment reflects the severity of the presentation's failure to support the ambitious claims. The novelty and impact scores remain at 2, reflecting the consensus that the idea is a repackaging of existing concepts with inflated, causally disconnected impact projections.
final.uncertainty: The primary uncertainty lies in the potential for the proposal to be re-scoped as a purely symbolic awareness campaign rather than a systemic solution. If the long-term impact claims (solar laws, bus electrification) were removed and the scope limited to the immediate, short-term behavioral nudge, the feasibility and impact scores might improve slightly, though the novelty would remain low. Additionally, the specific costs of security and health support for the estimated 145,000 participants remain unquantified, leaving the true financial barrier to entry undefined. The divergence between S2's slightly higher presentation score and S1's lower score was resolved by prioritizing the critical flaws in data integrity and citation, which undermine the proposal's credibility with the high-level stakeholders required for success.
